Orna Resnekov

Methods development, mass spectrometry

oresnekov@molsci.org
510-981-8747 (w)

Roles
Research Research@MSI
Research The Alpha Project

Biography
Education:
B.S. English, 1981
M.S. Biology, 1983, University of Michigan, Ann Arbor.
Ph.D. Virology, 1989, Weizmann Institute of Science, Rehovot, Israel.
Thesis Title: "Transcription Termination in Animal Viruses and Cells".


Postdoctoral studies:
1988-1991 Karolinska Institute, Stockholm, Sweden.
1991-1997 Bunting Institute Science Scholar and Postdoctoral fellow, Harvard University, Cambridge.


Selected Awards & Fellowships:
1989 The Dr. Ester Halinger Prize for Ph.D. studies.
1991-1992 Science Scholar, Bunting Institute of Radcliffe College.
1990-1991 Swedish Cancer Society.
1988-1990 European Molecular Biology Organization Postdoctoral Fellowship.


Professional Experience:
1998-2000 Senior Staff Fellow, NIH-National Institute of Child Health and Human Development, Section on Microbial Genetics.
2000 Joined TMSI, Berkeley, Ca.
2002 Co-investigator, CEGS at TMSI
2003 Assistant Research Director and Alpha Project Manager


Current Research:

Co-investigator, the Alpha Project

My main focus is the development of genome-scale population measurement technologies to quantify proteins and their modifications in cells. I am presently concentrating on understanding information flow in yeast cells through the quantification of phosphorylation states of proteins in the Alpha-factor pathway using mass spectrometry. In this work I develop and use technology, analyze results, train researchers and graduate students, coordinate the efforts of my collaborators (Dr. Robert Maxwell, Dr. David Camp and Dr. Richard Smith, Pacific Northwest National Laboratory) and their coworkers, and make data publicly available on the TMSI Alpha Project website. Results from these approaches are also incorporated into computer simulations of the Alpha factor pathway. In this interdisciplinary work I work closely with Dr. Lok (TMSI), Dr. Endy (MIT), and Dr. Bruck (CalTech). I previously worked on the Gram-positive microorganism, Bacillus subtilis where I acquired extensive experience studying signal transduction using genetics, protein purification and the analysis and use of GFP derivatives. At TMSI, I also champion the inclusion of greater segments of our population in genomic research through two Center-based programs, the Education and Training Plan and the Underrepresented Minority Recruitment, Training and Outreach Plan.

Publications

Resnekov, O, Rutberg, L, von, Gabain A. Changes in the stability of specific mRNA species in response to growth stage in Bacillus subtilis. PNAS 1990, 87, 8355-8359 PubMed
